name: Further-Enhanced Type II Port Selection (feType II PS)

lineage.adds: free (any-combination) port selection and a tiny reciprocity-based delay window

tagline: When the tower already knows the delays from the uplink, the phone barely needs to mention frequency at all — just say which ports matter, freely, and one or two taps.
overview: In a **TDD (time-division duplex)** network, uplink and downlink share the same frequency, so the base station (**gNB**) can often measure the channel's delays directly from what the phone sends it — a property called **delay reciprocity**. If the gNB already knows the delays, why should the phone (**UE**) spend bits re-describing them? Further-enhanced Type II port selection is built around exactly that shortcut: it pre-compensates the bulk delay so the dominant energy sits at delay-tap zero, and the UE only needs to report at most **one extra tap** ($M \in \{1,2\}$) within a small window — instead of R16's search across a handful of taps spread over the whole band.

The other big change is the spatial report. R16's port-selection variant picked a *consecutive, strided* run of ports; R17 makes port selection **free** — the UE may choose any $L$ of the $P/2$ ports per polarization, encoded with a general combinatorial index (Algorithm 4). This is a strictly more flexible spatial report, well suited to CSI-RS that is already beamformed onto a scattered set of strong ports rather than a tidy consecutive block.

Configuration runs through the parameter triple $(M, \alpha, \beta)$: $\alpha$ sets what fraction of the ports are used ($K_1 = \alpha P$), $M$ is the tap count, and $\beta$ sets the coefficient budget fraction. When $\alpha = 1$ all ports are used and the port-selection index isn't even reported. Everything else — the amplitude/phase quantization tables, the bitmap, the strongest-coefficient convention — is inherited unchanged from R16 (eType II); only the spatial basis and the tap window differ.

An interesting and counter-intuitive property falls out of this design: because the port budget $K_1$ grows with the array size $P$, a **larger array gives R17 more ports to combine and proportionally more coefficient budget** — so its reconstruction accuracy can actually *improve* as the array grows, the opposite of the usual "bigger array, harder to feed back" intuition. This chapter's codebook also underlies the R18 predicted port-selection variant, which reuses this exact machinery for a single future time interval.

howItWorks.title: UE measures the channel and aligns per-port phase
howItWorks.body: The phone estimates the channel via CSI-RS (channel state information reference signal) and phase-aligns each port's per-subband coefficient to a common reference.

howItWorks.title: UE freely selects the strongest ports
howItWorks.body: Instead of picking a consecutive strided run, the UE ranks all P/2 ports per polarization by accumulated energy and keeps the L strongest, encoding the chosen set with a general combinatorial index.

howItWorks.title: UE finds at most one extra delay tap
howItWorks.body: Tap 0 is always assumed present (the reciprocity reference). If the configuration allows a second tap, the UE searches only a tiny window (of size N) for the strongest additional delay.

howItWorks.title: UE quantizes amplitude, phase, and the coefficient bitmap
howItWorks.body: Exactly the R16 machinery: a per-polarization reference amplitude, a per-coefficient differential amplitude, a 16-PSK (phase-shift keying) phase, and a bitmap marking which of the port/tap coefficients survive the budget.

howItWorks.title: Report travels over the uplink
howItWorks.body: The freely-chosen port combination, at most one tap offset, the bitmap, and the quantized coefficients form the PMI (precoding matrix indicator).

howItWorks.title: gNB reconstructs using its own known delays and transmits
howItWorks.body: The base station combines the reported port weights with the tiny reported delay structure (leaning on its own reciprocity-derived delay knowledge) to rebuild the full per-subband precoder, then transmits.

whatIsReported.plain: Which L ports (out of P/2) were freely selected, per polarization.
whatIsReported.detail: A general combinatorial index (Algorithm 4) over any subset of ports — not a fixed stride — reported only when alpha < 1 (i.e. not all ports are used).

whatIsReported.plain: The offset of the second delay tap, if a second tap is used.
whatIsReported.detail: Reported only when M=2 and the window size N=4; identifies which of a few nearby delay offsets carries the second tap's energy.

whatIsReported.plain: A bitmap marking which port/tap coefficients are nonzero, per layer.
whatIsReported.detail: Same role as R16's bitmap, now over the freely selected ports and the (at most 2) delay taps.

whatIsReported.plain: Which coefficient is the strongest, per layer.
whatIsReported.detail: A flat index over port and tap position identifying the reference coefficient, whose amplitude and phase are then fixed by convention.

whatIsReported.plain: The per-polarization reference amplitude, per layer.
whatIsReported.detail: Same 4-bit-per-polarization mechanism as R16; only the non-strongest polarization's value is actually reported.

whatIsReported.plain: The differential amplitude for each kept coefficient.
whatIsReported.detail: 3-bit refinement per reported coefficient, identical table to R16.

whatIsReported.plain: The 16-PSK phase for each kept coefficient.
whatIsReported.detail: 4-bit phase index per reported coefficient, identical table to R16.

parametersExplained.plain: A preset (1-8) bundling the tap count M, the port fraction alpha, and the coefficient budget fraction beta.
parametersExplained.guidance: Higher combinations use more ports and/or a richer coefficient budget; combinations 1 and 6 are barred at very small arrays (4 or 12 ports) because they'd require a non-integer or odd port count.

parametersExplained.plain: The size of the small window the second delay tap is searched within, when a second tap is used.
parametersExplained.guidance: Only matters when the chosen parameter combination uses M=2 taps.

strengths: Extremely cheap frequency-domain report — at most one extra tap, thanks to reciprocity-based delay pre-compensation.
Free (unrestricted) port selection captures scattered strong ports that a fixed-stride scheme would miss.
Accuracy can improve, not degrade, as the antenna array grows larger.
Reuses R16's proven amplitude/phase/bitmap machinery, so it stays easy to reconstruct.
limitations: Assumes TDD-style delay reciprocity — the gNB already knowing the delays is the whole premise, so it is less natural for FDD (frequency-division duplex) deployments.
Only meaningful when the CSI-RS is already beamformed or port-reduced; it has no DFT beam search of its own.
The R=2 subband-pairing factor is validated but not applied to the reconstructed frequency-unit count.
The spec's "skip the bitmap when fully populated" optimization is not applied, so the bitmap is always sent in full even in that rare case.
whenToUse: Use feType II PS in TDD deployments where uplink/downlink reciprocity means the gNB already has good delay knowledge, and where CSI-RS is concentrated on a scattered set of ports rather than a full DFT beam search being useful.

mathHighlight.caption: The reconstructed coefficient sums over at most two delay taps and the freely selected ports, with tap 0 always present as the reciprocity-compensated reference.
